Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity:
Ralph E. Pierce, a 78-year-old male, is being admitted to home health care following a recent right total hip arthroplasty performed on February 26, 2026. This surgical intervention was
necessitated by significant pain that impaired his activities of daily living, despite previous conservative treatments. Post-operatively, Mr. Pierce demonstrates functional limitations, including
decreased mobility and difficulty ambulating without assistance, necessitating a structured rehabilitation program. Mr. Pierce requires skilled nursing services to monitor his surgical site for signs
of infection, manage pain effectively, and ensure adherence to prescribed medications, including anticoagulation therapy with Eliquis to mitigate his risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE).
Additionally, he will benefit from physical therapy services to facilitate recovery through therapeutic exercises, manual therapy, and modalities as indicated in his post-operative care plan, with
recommendation for therapy 2-3 times per week for 6-8 weeks.
The medical necessity for home health services is underscored by his recent hospital discharge, chronic illness management related to his osteoarthritis, and the need for ongoing rehabilitation
due to his advanced age and existing comorbidities, including a history of chronic pain and cardiovascular concerns. These factors collectively justify the need for home health admission,
ensuring that Mr. Pierce receives comprehensive and coordinated care in the comfort of his home, promoting optimal recovery outcomes and enhancing his quality of life.
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1 - no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
PIERCE, RALPH I 96 I DOB: 11/12/1947 I N: 02/28/2026
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2: the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 - Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1- Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3 - Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.

3. Unable to take medication unless administered by another person

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed): (Mark all that apply.
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated

Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects

Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing

(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury

Carry out patient specific interventions as appropriate/ordered, if patient is at risk for hospitalization: (Coordinate with M2250)
Consider implementing any of the following interventions, if patient is at risk for hospitalization:
Assess patient's: knowledge, ability, resources and adherence
Enrollment into a disease management program
Mobility: Use a walker as prescribed; follow hip precautions; wear supportive shoes; use safe transfer techniques.
Strength & Balance: PT to evaluate and treat; perform home exercises for lower extremity strength, core stability, and balance.
Medication & Health: Monitor for dizziness, hypotension, pain, swelling, or bleeding; adjust mobility as needed.
Education: Teach patient and caregiver about fall risks, safe transfers, and post-fall actions
(M1033) Risk for Hospitalization: Which of the following signs or symptoms characterize this patient as at risk for hospitalization? (Mark all that apply.)
1. History of falls (2 or more falls - or any fall with an injury - in the past 12 months)
2. Unintentional weight loss of a total of 10 pounds or more in the past 12 months
3. Multiple hospitalizations (2 or more) in the past 6 months
4. Multiple emergency department visits (2 or more) in the past 6 months
5. Decline in mental, emotional, or behavioral status in the past 3 months
6. Reported or observed history of difficulty complying with any medical instructions (for example, medications, diet, exercise) in the past 3 months
Prior history of falls within 3 months
An unintentional change in position resulting in coming to rest on the ground or at a lower level
Inability to make it to the bathroom or commode in timely manner.
Includes but not limited to, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathies, visual field loss, age related changes, decline in visual acuity,
accommodation, glare tolerance, depth perception, and night vision or not wearing prescribed glasses or having the correct prescription.
May include patients who need help with IADLS or ADLS or have gait or transfer problems, arthritis, pain, fear of falling, foot problems,
impaired sensation, impaired coordination or improper use of assistive devices.
May include but not limited to, poor illumination, equipment tubing, inappropriate footwear, pets, hard to reach items, floor surfaces that
are uneven or cluttered, or outdoor entry and exits.
Poly Pharmacy (4 or more prescriptions any type)
All PRESCRIPTIONS including prescriptions for OTC meds. Drugs highly associated with fall risk include but not limited to, sedatives, antidepressants,
tranquilizers, narcotics, antihypertensives, cardiac meds, corticosteroids, anti-anxiety drugs, anticholinergic drugs, and
Pain often affects an individual's desire or ability to move or pain can be a factor in depression or compliance with safety recommendations.
Could include patients with dementia, Alzheimer's or stroke patients or patients who are confused, use poor judgment, have decreased
comprehension, impulsivity, memory deficits. Consider patients ability to adhere to the plan of care.
A score of 4 or more is considered at risk for falling

device such as cane, crutch, or walker) prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such
as shopping or remembering to take medication prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury.
Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided
03. Partial/moderate assistance Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns

PLAN OF CARE AND SKILLED INTERVENTIONS
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
ADMIT PATIENT TO STAR HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
A 78-year-old male patient was admitted to home health services following recent right hip joint replacement surgery with presence of an artificial hip joint, presenting with postoperative pain,
generalized weakness, limited mobility, impaired ambulation requiring a walker, difficulty with transfers, dependence with toileting, need for assistance with grooming and dressing, daily bowel
incontinence, open decubitus ulcers requiring assessment, dorsalgia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, essential hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, moderate nutritional risk, complex medication
regimen including anticoagulants, opioids, and hypoglycemics, and increased fall risk related to decreased strength and functional limitations.
Skilled nurse services ordered to provide comprehensive assessment, medication management and safety review, pain management support, assessment of skin integrity and decubitus
ulcers, bowel management guidance, diabetic and chronic disease management support, nutritional status reinforcement, fall prevention strategies, and caregiver support and instruction to
promote recovery, prevent complications, and maintain safety in home environment. Physical therapy services ordered to address impaired mobility, decreased strength and endurance,

balance deficits, gait instability requiring assistive device, and limitations with transfers and activities of daily living following joint replacement surgery. PT to provide therapeutic exercises, gait
training with walker, balance and strengthening activities, transfer training, and functional mobility training to improve independence with ambulation and daily activities, reduce fall risk, and
help patient achieve highest possible functional level while recovering safely at home.
Skilled Nurse: Once a week for 1 week, then twice a week for the next 3 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication review and reconciliation, assess level of pain, care &
management of surgical wound on right hip, infection control measures, compliance with treatment regimen and therapeutic diet, home safety and disease process teaching.
Physical Therapy: Evaluation and treatment for therapeutic exercise, strengthening, mobility, gait, transfer and fall prevention techniques training.
Patient's goal: "I want my wound to heal without complications, get stronger and better, and be able to perform activities of daily living independently."
Patient Covid-19 screening completed prior to visit. No report of symptoms or known COVID-19 exposure per patient/family. Patient provided with mask for use during HH visit. Patient agreed to use
mask during treatment. Clinician performed hand hygiene, donned mask, face shield and gloves and followed infection control and bag technique procedures during the visit. No COVID related
concerns during the time of visit. Disinfection of equipment followed including dry time. Wound care, proper disposal of wastes and doffing were done upon completion of the visit.

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment of all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharge medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with
patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed the plan of care draft with the patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial
discussion on the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.
1. Patient health status and homebound reason:
Ralph E. Pierce, a 78-year-old male, is being admitted to home health care following a recent right total hip arthroplasty performed on February 26, 2026. This surgical intervention was necessitated

by significant pain that impaired his activities of daily living, despite previous conservative treatments. Post-operatively, Mr. Pierce demonstrates functional limitations, including decreased mobility

and difficulty ambulating without assistance, necessitating a structured rehabilitation program. Mr. Pierce requires skilled nursing services to monitor his surgical site for signs of infection, manage

pain effectively, and ensure adherence to prescribed medications, including anticoagulation therapy with Eliquis to mitigate his risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE). Additionally, he will benefit from

physical therapy services to facilitate recovery through therapeutic exercises, manual therapy, and modalities as indicated in his post-operative care plan, with a recommendation for therapy 2-3 times
The medical necessity for home health services is underscored by his recent hospital discharge, chronic illness management related to his osteoarthritis, and the need for ongoing rehabilitation due
to his advanced age and existing comorbidities, including a history of chronic pain and cardiovascular concerns. These factors collectively justify the need for home health admission, ensuring that
Mr. Pierce receives comprehensive and coordinated care in the comfort of his home, promoting optimal recovery outcomes and enhancing his quality of life.

Skilled nurse discussed with the patient/caregiver the essential aftercare practices following joint replacement surgery, focusing on safe mobility, protection of the new joint, and supporting recovery at
home. SN explained the importance of following movement precautions specific to the replaced joint, such as avoiding sudden twisting, excessive bending, or crossing the legs, and highlighted how
maintaining proper body alignment during sitting, standing, and lying down could help prevent strain on the joint. SN walked through safe ways to move from bed to chair, use assistive devices like
walkers or canes as recommended, and gradually increase activity based on comfort and tolerance. Skilled nurse also covered the importance of taking prescribed medications as directed,
maintaining a balanced diet and adequate hydration to support healing, and allowing time for rest between activities to avoid overexertion. SN reviewed signs that may require contacting a healthcare
provider, such as increasing pain, swelling, redness, fever, or sudden difficulty moving the joint, and explained that keeping follow-up appointments with the surgeon or therapy team helps track
recovery progress and ensure the joint continues to heal properly.
Pain in the right hip with a scale of 5 out of 10; comfort measures and pain medication were provided. Re-checked after 30 minutes, resulting with a good relief. Skilled nurse discussed pain
management with the patient/caregiver and explained different ways to manage discomfort using both medication and non-medication approaches. The SN reviewed the importance of taking
prescribed pain medications as directed and highlighted how staying consistent with the recommended schedule could help keep pain under better control rather than waiting until discomfort
becomes severe. Skilled nurse explained common side effects that may occur with pain medications and brought up simple ways to reduce discomfort such as taking medication with food if
appropriate and allowing time for the medication to take effect. The SN also covered non-medication methods that may help relieve pain, including changing positions regularly, using proper body
support with pillows, applying relaxation techniques, and pacing activities throughout the day to avoid overexertion. The skilled nurse talked through the value of balancing activity with rest and
outlined that combining these approaches may help improve comfort and support overall recovery.
SN discussed fall preventive measures with the patient/caregiver to help reduce the risk of injury at home and during daily activities. SN reviewed common situations that can increase the chance of
falling and highlighted the importance of keeping walkways clear of clutter, loose rugs, and electrical cords to create a safer environment. The skilled nurse explained the value of adequate lighting
throughout the home, especially in hallways, bathrooms, and near the bed, to improve visibility during movement. SN walked through safe mobility habits such as rising slowly from sitting or lying
positions, wearing properly fitted non-slip footwear, and using assistive devices like canes or walkers when needed for added stability. The skilled nurse also covered the importance of placing
frequently used items within easy reach to avoid unnecessary bending or climbing and discussed using handrails on stairs and grab bars in key areas of the home to support balance and confidence
Patient verbalized 70% of understanding on the disease process taught during this visit. Patient able to teach back 6 out of 10 about the essential aftercare practices following joint replacement
surgery, pain management and fall preventive measures. Patient also redemonstrated 65% of home safety and fall prevention measures with the use of assistive device as per patient still
demonstrates weakness, poor endurance and slow ambulation/unsteady gait. Caregiver verbalized 75% of understanding and is available to assist patient for safety with ADLs/IADLs, medication and
SOC, POC, Visit frequencies and update of patient health condition

Ralph Pierce is a 78-year-old male patient who has been referred for home health care services on February 24, 2026. He is under the care of attending physician Michael Domer. The
discipline completing the assessment is nursing, with the start of care date recorded as February 28, 2026. Ralph was discharged from a short-stay acute hospital facility on February 27, 2026,
following a recent joint replacement surgery. His preferred language is English.
Ralph is considered homebound due to several factors related to his recent surgery. His status post-total right hip arthroplasty on February 26, 2026, has severely limited his ability to leave the
home. The criteria for his homebound status include illness, the contraindication of leaving home due to medical reasons, and the significant effort required for any outings. He experiences
weakness and pain from the surgery, which restricts his mobility and makes leaving home exhausting and potentially dangerous.
The primary reason for Ralph's need for home health services is aftercare following his joint replacement surgery. His primary diagnosis is Z47.1, indicating the need for continued rehabilitation
and monitoring post-surgery. Additionally, he has several comorbidities, including Z96.641 (presence of right artificial hip joint), M54.9 (dorsalgia unspecified), E11.9 (type 2 diabetes mellitus
without complications), I10 (essential hypertension), and E78.00 (pure hypercholesterolemia).
A comprehensive head-to-toe assessment reveals that Ralph's cardiopulmonary function is stable, with vital signs indicating a temperature of 97.4°F, a pulse of 78 beats per minute, blood
pressure of 110/68 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute. Gastrointestinal assessment shows no significant issues, but he does have daily bowel incontinence. The
integumentary system assessment indicates no pressure ulcers or surgical wounds, although he has open decubitus ulcers that require monitoring.
Ralph's psychosocial status indicates that he lives with other individuals in the home but requires training and support from non-agency caregivers. His cognitive function is assessed as alert
and oriented, with no signs of confusion or cognitive decline. He has a full understanding of his care needs and can follow instructions.
In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), Ralph requires assistance with grooming and dressing, although he can bathe with supervision. He is dependent on others for toileting and has
difficulty transferring independently. His mobility is significantly limited, necessitating the use of a walker and assistance for ambulation. His functional ability is categorized as independent in
self-care, but he requires supervision for many tasks.
Ralph's social needs include support from caregivers who may require additional training to assist him properly. He does not have any assistive devices aside from a walker at present. His
medication regimen is complex, with a total of five or more medications that may pose a risk for hospitalization. The medications include anticoagulants, opioids, and hypoglycemics, all of which
require careful monitoring for potential adverse effects and interactions.
Ralph's primary caregiver is a family member who has been involved in his care. Additional support systems in place include community resources and emergency preparedness plans
established for his safety. His nutritional health is a concern, with a moderate nutritional status score indicating risks related to his dietary needs.
In summary, Ralph's need for skilled home health services is medically necessary due to his recent surgery, complex medication management, and the requirement for supervision and
assistance with daily activities. The visit frequency for skilled nursing services will be determined based on his ongoing recovery needs and the assessment of functional improvements, with a
focus on rehabilitation and support for his homebound status. Regular monitoring and intervention are essential for preventing complications and ensuring a safe recovery at home.
